title: Learning and Testing k-Model Distributions
speaker: Rocco Servidio
abstract: A k-modal probability distribution over the domain {1,...,N} is one whose histogram has at most k "peaks" and "valleys". Such distributions are a natural generalization of the well-studied class of monotone increasing (or monotone decreasing) probability distributions.

proof_stages.label: Problem formulation, baselines, and main bounds
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines learning and identity testing for k-modal distributions over an ordered finite domain, contrasts them with unstructured distribution learning/testing, states the main sample-complexity upper and lower bounds, and situates the results relative to prior monotone/unimodal work.

proof_stages.label: Two sampling tools: DKW and empirical PDF learning
proof_stages.summary: The Dvoretzky--Kiefer--Wolfowitz inequality is introduced as a way to form approximate equal-probability intervals, and the standard empirical-estimator bound is recalled for learning an arbitrary distribution on a finite set.

proof_stages.label: Monotone case via Birgé's oblivious decomposition
proof_stages.summary: For k=0, Birgé's exponentially growing oblivious buckets make every monotone distribution close to its bucketwise flattening, reducing learning and identity testing to the corresponding arbitrary-distribution problems on only O(log n/epsilon) buckets, with a matching lower-bound sketch.

proof_stages.label: Naive general-k learner and its extra epsilon factor
proof_stages.summary: A first k-modal learner partitions the domain into many small-mass intervals, runs monotone learners on all of them, and combines the hypotheses, but its sample complexity loses a factor because the learner is invoked on about k/epsilon intervals.

proof_stages.label: Promised k-modal monotonicity tester
proof_stages.summary: The needed tester distinguishes whether an unknown promised k-modal distribution is monotone or epsilon-far from monotone using O(k^2/epsilon^2) samples, by checking interval-average inequalities and proving soundness through local peak-and-valley corrections.

proof_stages.label: Semi-agnostic monotone learner
proof_stages.summary: Birgé's oblivious-bucket learner is observed to remain effective when the target is only OPT-close to monotone, returning a hypothesis with error on the order of OPT plus epsilon, which is necessary because a tester can certify only approximate monotonicity.

proof_stages.label: Sharp general-k learning algorithm
proof_stages.summary: The final learner uses DKW to form small initial intervals, repeatedly extends a block while the promised monotonicity tester accepts and marks spoiler intervals when it rejects, then agnostically learns the resulting close-to-monotone superintervals and ignores the at most k marked low-mass intervals.

proof_stages.label: Identity testing for known k-modal distributions and outlook
proof_stages.summary: The known-q identity tester is sketched by decomposing according to small intervals and q's modes, applying the monotonicity/identity checks locally, and then the talk concludes with the testing-versus-learning separation and future directions for broader and more structured distribution classes.

examples.name: Sketched monotone and k-modal histograms
examples.note: The speaker draws increasing, unimodal, and three-modal histograms to make the definition of k-modality concrete, without deriving any later argument from the particular shapes.

examples.name: Uniform distribution versus uniform on a random subset
examples.note: This sample-indistinguishability construction explains why testing whether an arbitrary unknown distribution is k-modal is too hard and later motivates retaining the k-modal promise in the monotonicity tester.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker uses the uniform-vs-random-subset case to reason through a lower bound, comparing modality/farness and explaining why samples cannot distinguish them because collisions are unlikely

passes.reason: The maximally regular uniform distribution is paired with uniform-on-a-random-subset as an indistinguishable hard instance for k-modality testing.
The uniform-vs-random-subset construction is an indistinguishability/lower-bound enemy case, with the full uniform distribution being maximally regular.
the uniform-vs-random-subset pair is an adversarial indistinguishability example showing the harder testing problem cannot be easy
The uniform-vs-random-subset pair is a hard-instance obstruction showing that testing k-modality without the known-reference promise is sample-hard, with the uniform side maximally regular.
the full uniform distribution is maximally regular and the pair is used as an indistinguishability lower-bound obstruction to testing k-modality
the uniform-vs-random-subset pair is an adversarial lower-bound example for testing k-modality, with the full uniform distribution maximally regular
The pair is used as an indistinguishability lower-bound obstruction, with the full uniform distribution being maximally symmetric.

examples.name: Heavy atom obstruction to equal-mass bucketing
examples.note: A point mass larger than the target bucket mass is used as an edge-case illustration of why DKW-based partitions are only morally equal-mass and how heavy points can be handled separately.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker analyzes the heavy-point case as an obstruction to equal-mass intervals and explains how repeated samples would identify such atoms so they can be handled separately

passes.reason: A heavy atom is used as the obstruction showing exact equal-mass interval bucketing can fail.

examples.name: Sum of independent biased coins
examples.note: The Poisson-binomial distribution of the number of heads from n coins with unknown biases illustrates a more structured unimodal subclass learnable with sample complexity independent of n.
